title: Cat Vomiting White Foam: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat vomiting white foam? Learn what it means, when to call a vet, and how to triage your pet's symptoms for a fast recovery.
content_html:
TL;DR: White foam in a cat's vomit usually signifies an empty stomach or mild gastric irritation, but veterinary consultation is necessary if it occurs more than twice in 24 hours or is accompanied by lethargy.

What does it mean if my cat is vomiting white foam?

White foam in a cat's vomit is typically a mixture of saliva and gastric juices that has been aerated by the action of vomiting. Because there is no food present, the liquid appears bubbly or frothy. This often occurs when a cat has an empty stomach or is experiencing mild gastrointestinal irritation.

Is it an emergency if my cat vomits white foam?

  • The urgency level for this condition is generally Medium.
  • A single episode may not be cause for alarm.
  • Consult a veterinarian if the vomiting persists more than twice in 24 hours.
  • Seek care if your cat seems lethargic or refuses to eat.
  • Treat as an immediate emergency if accompanied by distress, gasping, or a known ingestion of a toxin.

Why should I take a photo of my cat's vomit for the vet?

  • A visual record allows your veterinary team to assess the volume and exact consistency.
  • Photos capture subtle tinting, such as yellow or pink, that might otherwise be missed.
  • This helps your vet distinguish between simple stomach upset, hairballs, or more serious conditions like esophageal issues.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, vomiting white foam can be associated with various underlying causes, including dietary indiscretion or intolerance (ingestion of novel, spoiled, or contaminated foods, or foreign material), parasitic infection, bacterial or viral gastroenteritis, anxiety, motion sickness, drug or toxin ingestion (antibiotics, NSAIDs, corticosteroids, plants, chemicals), systemic illness (pancreatitis, uremic gastropathy, hypoadrenocorticism), or endoparasitism (Ollulanus sp). Diagnostic evaluation includes a thorough history, clinical findings, and response to symptomatic treatment. If vomiting persists, reevaluation is warranted, including abdominal palpation to assess for foreign bodies or discomfort. Examination of the oral cavity to check for yarn, thread, or needles, and abdominal radiographs to look for radiopaque foreign objects or signs of intestinal distention indicative of obstruction, are important.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 390)

faqs.question: Can a hairball cause white foam?
faqs.answer: Yes, cats often vomit white foam if a hairball is irritating the stomach lining but has not yet been expelled.

faqs.question: When should I be worried about my cat's vomiting?
faqs.answer: Seek veterinary care if the vomiting is frequent, if there is blood, or if your cat is acting weak, hiding, or refusing water.

faqs.question: Should I remove my cat's food after they vomit foam?
faqs.answer: It is often recommended to pick up food for a few hours to let the stomach settle, but you should always consult your vet first to ensure it is safe for your specific cat.
